title: Religious symbolism in Eva or the lack thereof
display_title: Religious symbolism in Eva or the lack thereof
wikitext:
[]: Religious symbolism in Eva or the lack thereof             Quote Post by chaosakita » Sun Sep 15, 2013 4:57 am [Spiritual sequel to this thread . - Monk] My friend refuses to believe Anno's explanation of how the Christian symbols found in Eva are mostly window dressing. I probably can't change his mind, but it'd be great hearing some explanations on why he most likely isn't right.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Sun Sep 15, 2013 5:04 am In what way does he not believe Anno's explanation? Does he think Anno was using the religious imagery to communicate meaning unconsciously, including meaning Anno did not himself intend but which might still exist (delivered by "society" or the factors which motivated Anno to include the imagery or what-have-you)? Because I don't see how you can exactly prove someone wrong that the message they received was an actual message if they see the author as only the deliverer of the message and not its originator. Some people see "God" in the pattern of falling leaves, and how the hell do you prove them wrong, let alone someone who sees meaning in the usage of religious imagery in fiction regardless of the statements of the author that it's window dressing?

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sun Sep 15, 2013 6:20 am Much visual symbolism and metaphor is mainly "window-dressing" anyway. I can't think of an example where visual symbolism addressed an elements that wasn't already explored (or setting up to be explored) through other methods anyway. So, I guess the real question is if these things were symbolizing anything to begin with. I guess one can offer sacrifice as the "meaning" behind the image of the cross seeing as how Misato has it after her dad sacrificed himself for her, and it appears every time people die as the Angels prepare for 3 Impact. But then, even the Christian symbolism behind the cross isn't endorsed by Scripture at all. I mean, if Christians are willing to have the cross mean something other than Roman rule and tyranny r persecution by the State, which Jesus says we must be willing to "pick up daily" if needed to order to follow Him, then I guess the cross can also mean anything that Anno wants it to mean/not mean.

[]:             Quote Post by chaosakita » Sun Sep 15, 2013 3:18 pm He says Anno is "lying" about the explanation and states that there's some higher meaning to the cross-shaped explosions, the enemies being named Angels, etc. And he states Shinjji as the case of being "the prodigal son" (although that's blatantly not the case).

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Sun Sep 15, 2013 3:37 pm While Tsurumaki did say: There are a lot of giant robot shows in Japan, and we did want our story to have a religious theme to help distinguish us. Because Christianity is an uncommon religion in Japan we thought it would be mysterious. None of the staff who worked on Eva are Christians. There is no actual Christian meaning to the show, we just thought the visual symbols of Christianity look cool. If we had known the show would get distributed in the US and Europe we might have rethought that choice.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sun Sep 15, 2013 3:38 pm One has to remember that all of maybe 2% of Japanese citizens profess to be born-again Christians. As a result, any use of Christians iconography in their media is almost always window dressing, regardless of how much thought was put into it, simply because they cannot relate to it the same way most Western people can. If they were really trying to present something meaningful to their culture, they would have used Shinto Buddhist imagery instead, or at least something else the Japanese people can better relate to. EDIT: Also, what Eva Monkey said.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sun Sep 15, 2013 3:42 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: The Angels exploding into crosses was deliberate, but meaningless,

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Sun Sep 15, 2013 3:46 pm Chuckman wrote: No they aren't. All of the angels are being sacrificed so humanity might live, but Kaworu is the only one who can talk and point this out.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Sun Sep 15, 2013 3:50 pm Personally, i think it's very telling that Tsurumaki said the religious symbolism had no *christian* meaning, and that if they ever thought that NGE would get as far as the west (where social autism about these matters was likely to erupt) they probably wouldn't have included it. Whenever i check old threads, i tend to agree with EvaYojimbo's general interpretation of the religious symbolism. IIRC he said something along the lines of symbolisms like the cross or jesus parallels to be more of an evocation or appropiation of that particular element of christian faith, and extracting it's potential narrative value to the story he wanted to tell. Of course i'm probably misremembering and he surely was much more elaborate in his opinion, but i think those are my personal feelings on these matters. Ultimately, i've always seen the drama with the christian elements and staff's treatment of it (including the Tsurumaki quote) as something to some degree similar to the Aum Shinrikyo event. They were elements in the series that, for reasons outside Gainax's control, would end up drawing attention and controversy due to the fact they would be interpreted in ways Anno never intended, and thus they decided to just abandon them (Tsurumaki quote) so that they wouldn't end up hogging all the attention to the series and thus undermine Anno's intentions with Eva.

[]:             Quote Post by chaosakita » Sun Sep 15, 2013 4:00 pm Chuckman wrote: No they aren't. All of the angels are being sacrificed so humanity might live, but Kaworu is the only one who can talk and point this out.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sun Sep 15, 2013 4:07 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: Sacrificed by whom? They're not actually divine beings, they're just the progeny of another seed of alien life.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Sun Sep 15, 2013 4:13 pm It's probably worth mentioning that Anno added the "Humanity fighting and putting their entire will into their survival and right to exist" motif in Gunbuster, and at least two times it's given overt/primary attention in episodes 3 and 6.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Sun Sep 15, 2013 4:30 pm They be dyin' and shit. Their death is required for humanity to survive. I hate the brutally boring demystification that comes from the paratext. Oh, ancient aliens, how wonderful and original.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sun Sep 15, 2013 4:37 pm AuraTwilight wrote: I think trying to connect the crosses to 'sacrifice' loses its symbolic meaning when the non-Kaworu Angels aren't MARTYRS. Just my 2 cents.

[]:             Quote Post by chaosakita » Sun Sep 15, 2013 4:42 pm Chuckman wrote: Look at Dream's comment above. The angels are also "fighting and putting their entire will into their survival and right to exist". Each is, in fact, an entire race attempting to do so. They simply attack with no effort at communication, at least a first... but humanity also just attacks them without any attempt to communicate. It's a commentary on the way aliens/robots/non-human entities are treated as perfectly acceptable targets of horrific violence in children's entertainment.

[]:             Quote Post by SimplyMason0 » Sun Sep 15, 2013 4:50 pm I did saw some heavy analysis here on the coma scene using obscure religious stuff. So I do question if Anno did some "bonuses" for people interested in that but still easy to understand for those that don't. Maybe he did it stealthy? Or it could be a fan putting things in that are coincidences.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sun Sep 15, 2013 6:11 pm Death is just a part of war in NGE, whether it be Angel death or Human death. Sure, that's technically sacrifice. But it's no more significant than the sacrificial death that was portrayed on screen without the cross to signify it. (Kaji sacrificed himself for the truth in NGE. Where's his cross?)

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Sun Sep 15, 2013 6:17 pm But nothing removes the fact that there is a reason why they're dying. That we don't have any gunbuster scenes of "Maybe we're just insects to the universe" doesn't change that one bit. We are getting off-topic.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sun Sep 15, 2013 6:20 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Death is just a part of war in NGE, whether it be Angel death or Human death. Sure, that's technically sacrifice. But it's no more significant than the sacrificial death that was portrayed on screen without the cross to signify it. (Kaji sacrificed himself for the truth in NGE. Where's his cross?)

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sun Sep 15, 2013 9:29 pm Hahaha! Wow. You'd have to not watch the series or something in order to miss the point of Kaji like that. Have we forgotten that Kaji actively pursued Misato, rendering any argument that Kaji was attempting to escape from Misato by finding the truth nonsensical at best? (Episode 8; Kaji: "But you're lips said 'Yes.' Which am I to believe? You're words or your lips?") But this thread is off topic enough as it is. I find it fascinating enough that people would argue that a mainly unchristian population and production staff were actually attempting something meaningful with Christian iconography; iconography that, as I've stated before, even Christians take out of the context of Scripture most of the time anyway. There is no Scriptural meaning one can apply to Evangelion, and the symbolism used in Eva, if it had any meaning contextual to the show alone, was used as a pretty-looking reminder of ideas developed in actual flashbacks and stuff at best. (Heck, Misato's scarred chest also symbolizes sacrifice. Does this make her a metaphoric savior as much as we try to paint Shinji as one well? Or does that role go to Lilith for actually being impaled by the Lance of Longinus ? Or does it mean anything outside of one of the most awesome phallic-looking plot devices in the entire series?)

I probably can't change his mind, but it'd be great hearing some explanations on why he most likely isn't right.
